step_1_context.text: Before plural and uncountable nouns we sometimes use some or zero article (i.e. no article) with very little difference in meaning:

step_2_explanation.simple_rule: Before plural and uncountable nouns we sometimes use some or zero article (i.
step_2_explanation.formula:
Some is used before a number to mean 'approximately':
 • Some eighty per cent of all residents took part in the vote.
step_2_explanation.common_mistake: When we don't know, can't remember, or it is not important exactly which person or thing we are talking about, we can use some instead of a / an with a singular noun.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 48.1.1 Sports are dangerous. (Correct this sentence if necessary)
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: When not referring to the whole class of things, we use 'some' to refer to a subset.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 48.2.1 250 people charged with assault following Molton riots. (Rewrite using 'some' to mean 'approximately')
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'Some' can be used before a number to mean approximately.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 48.3.1 I don't know where I got the information from. I must have heard it on ___ radio programme (or other).
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: When we don't remember exactly which one, we can use 'some' with a singular noun.

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 48.4.1 Elias worked hard at learning Japanese but failed to make ___ real progress.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: Use 'any' in a non-affirmative context (after 'failed to').

step_3_interactive_exercises.question: 48.4.2 I was unable to eat ___ of the food.
step_3_interactive_exercises.explanation_for_failure: 'any' means none of it; 'some' means you couldn't eat a portion of it.
